7. Group Convolutions

So far groups have acted on objects (atoms, vectors, displacement modes). Now considser groups acting on functions. There is a distinction to make between objects and functions:

  • Active transformations: objects move forward by
  • Passive transformations: functions on fixed coordinatesare updated by pulling the values back from .

For example, if you translate an object two units to the right, actively a feature at moves to . Passively, the new function representing the feature becomes .

So far our data has lived in vector spaces that groups act on (representations). For group convolution we adopt a different perspective: data as a function on the group itself. Standard convolution is convoluton on the translation group . For group convolution we replace with arbitrary group . E.g. for a CNN represents how strongly a certain feature is detected at position . For example, Later we’ll see that convolution can also be formulated for data the spaces the group acts on (e.g. on the sphere).

Remark. The two perspectives are connected through the regular representation. A funcction is an element of the vector space of the regular representation, which we know decomposes into irreps (we’ll see this recovers the representation-theoretic view)

7.1. Cross-Correlation

The fundamental operation in CNN is cross-correlation (abusively called convolution).

  • is the input signal (e.g. pixel values of an image)
  • is the filter/kernel.

7.3. Convolution Theorem for

Let’s first look at standard convolution and how we compute that efficiently using convolution theorem.

A 2D image is a function on . Translations in different dimensions commute, so the full translation group is a direct group . Hence we develop the theory for a single dimension and the multi-dimensional case follows by direct product.

is an abelian group and has 1-dimensional distinct irreps. We can identify it’s regular representation as corresponding to each shift, so that

(35)

Hence computing cross-correlation at every requires multiplying the regular representation for each group element. If we can find a basis that diagonalizes all these matrices simultaneously, then the computation is simpler. Since the group is abelian, the irrep basis works!

7.3.1. Finding the Irrep basis

Here are the irreps of . The generator must have order so it must be a root of unity. Hence the th irrep is

(36)

The eigenvectors of the shift matrix are the roots of unity , and the th eigenvector is

(37)

The change of basis matrix is .

7.4. Integration over the Group

A principle that will keep showing up: when you sum or integrate over the entire group, only the trivial irrep survives.

For a finite group , define the group average of a representation matrix by the orthogonality theorem.

Where this shows up:

  • Invariant output by averaging features over the group

7.6. Input Data

The discussion above assumes are data is a function of the group but to go from image data to group-indexed data we need to lift the input to the regular representation by creating a group channel and copying it times. My understanding is that lifting the image and some initial filter yields times applying the rotated filter to each image like Cohen-Welling do in their first layer.

For 2D images, two groups act simultaneously, the translation group and the point group or . The filter therefore lives in a tensor product

7.7. Group Convolution

The full convolution looks like

Remarks

  • Each of the filter is not related. Rrather the equivariance comes from matching with where .
  • In the full group convolution, it is not just “apply one filter to rotated copies of the image.” Cohen and welling do this in the first layer to get features. 8.750 instead does trivila-rep promotion.

7.9. [TODO] Nonlinearites and pooling

For equivariance with respect to a nonlinearity, we need

(40)

Pointwise nonlinearities commutes with the group action since the regular reprenstation is a permutation matrix. For non-permutation representations (e.g. the th column of the representation contains more than just a single nonzero entry, since ReLu(x+y) does not equal Relu(x) + Relu(y)) e.g. non-trivial irreps, pointwise nonlinearities break equivariance so we must use specially designed nonlinearities.
